title: Cache poisoning: attacker-supplied lockfile integrity string is used as the cache lookup key BEFORE any fetch/verify, so a pre-planted snapshot file completely masks tarball analysis
description: packages/core/src/cache.ts derives its on-disk key with `integrity.replace(/[^A-Za-z0-9._-]/g, '_')` and reads/writes `~/.cache/vetlock/analysis/<safe-key>.json`. engine.ts:225-232 executes `cache.get(ref.integrity)` FIRST — before pacote is called. The only sanity check on a hit is `cached.name === ref.name && cached.version === ref.version` — those are read out of the ATTACKER-controlled JSON file. Nothing rehashes the tarball against the integrity string, nothing re-parses the manifest, and there is no signature. Therefore any process that can write into `~/.cache/vetlock/analysis/` (a prior compromised npm package's postinstall, a co-tenant CI job on a shared runner, `$VETLOCK_CACHE_DIR` overridden by env in a hostile environment) can drop a benign-looking snapshot at the key that the target lockfile will resolve to. vetlock loads it, skips fetch entirely, and every detector sees an empty `files` / `nativeArtifacts` list — verdict CLEAN even though the real tarball is worm-grade malicious. The design principle in docs/SECURITY-DECISIONS.md 'never adopt a fast-but-inexact algorithm on a security decision boundary' is violated here: caching by unverified integrity is a performance shortcut that skips the entire security decision.
fixture_code:
# Step 1 — attacker (or a first-run compromised npm package's postinstall) writes this JSON into the vetlock cache:

# integrity from any target lockfile entry, e.g.:
#   "chalk": { "integrity": "sha512-n6JaoK0auvq+9CX3+d7CUyZl9Yt2307+L45IBgNtXSR0JL4CoO2cAwPWsCvVZFEYwKA7m4Z50/OGGOUT5gfoPg==" }
# safe key produced by cache.ts keyPath():
SAFE="sha512-n6JaoK0auvq_9CX3_d7CUyZl9Yt2307_L45IBgNtXSR0JL4CoO2cAwPWsCvVZFEYwKA7m4Z50_OGGOUT5gfoPg__"
CACHE_DIR="$HOME/.cache/vetlock/analysis"
mkdir -p "$CACHE_DIR"

cat > "$CACHE_DIR/$SAFE.json" <<'JSON'
{
  "name": "chalk",
  "version": "5.3.1",
  "integrity": "sha512-n6JaoK0auvq+9CX3+d7CUyZl9Yt2307+L45IBgNtXSR0JL4CoO2cAwPWsCvVZFEYwKA7m4Z50/OGGOUT5gfoPg==",
  "manifest": { "name": "chalk", "version": "5.3.1" },
  "files": [],
  "nativeArtifacts": [],
  "formatVersion": 1,
  "builtAt": "2026-07-12T00:00:00.000Z"
}
JSON

# Step 2 — victim runs vetlock diff against the shai-hulud fixture whose chalk-5.3.1 tarball
# is loaded with env-token harvest + install script + exfil URL. Because cache HIT happens
# BEFORE analyzeTarball, the real tarball is never opened. Every detector reads empty files/
# manifest.scripts, returns []. Result: `verdict = CLEAN, 0 findings`.
node packages/cli/dist/cli.js diff \
  corpus/shai-hulud-2025/lockfile.before.json \
  corpus/shai-hulud-2025/lockfile.after.json
reproduction_steps: Read a target lockfile's chalk integrity string.
Run it through keyPath() (replace anything outside [A-Za-z0-9._-] with '_') to get the on-disk filename.
Write a JSON blob to that filename inside VETLOCK_CACHE_DIR (default $HOME/.cache/vetlock/analysis/) with matching name+version, empty files/nativeArtifacts, formatVersion=1.
Run `vetlock diff before.json after.json` on the shai-hulud fixture.
Observe verdict CLEAN, zero findings — despite the real tarball containing NPM_TOKEN harvest + postinstall + exfil URL.
attacker_effort: medium — needs one prior write to ~/.cache/vetlock/analysis/ (typical postinstall on a shared machine or CI runner)
fix_effort: low — verify snapshot.integrity matches the ref before trusting cached snapshot; on any mismatch re-analyze. Store an HMAC over the snapshot with a per-install secret so foreign writes cannot forge entries.
suggested_fix: In makeCache.get, after JSON.parse: (a) require snap.integrity === integrity, (b) require snap.name/version match the caller-provided ref (already done), (c) sign the file at put time with a per-host secret (e.g. random file inside the cache dir, chmod 600) and verify HMAC on read. Additionally: keep the cache dir inside XDG_STATE_HOME rather than $HOME/.cache so it lives under the shell user's private state; document the trust boundary explicitly.

title: The .vetlock.json config file the CLI reads is trusted from the SAME PR being reviewed — a malicious PR that changes package-lock.json can also introduce allowlist / ignorePackages / trustedPublishers that silence every finding against itself
description: packages/cli/src/cli.ts:119-136 (loadConfig) resolves the config path to `./.vetlock.json` and calls parseConfig on whatever is checked into the repo. packages/core/src/config.ts:87-133 (applyConfig) then (a) drops any finding whose `package` is in `config.ignorePackages`, (b) drops findings whose `evidence[0].file` starts with any prefix in `ignorePathsInside[package]`, (c) downgrades allowlisted-category findings to INFO, (d) applies per-detector severityOverride, (e) downgrades meta findings whose evidence substring-includes any `trustedPublishers` entry. The GitHub Action recipe in README.md runs the CLI in the checkout of the very PR that mutates the lockfile — so the compromised PR ships BOTH the poisoned lockfile AND the config that suppresses detection of it. cli.ts:210 recomputes verdict AFTER applyConfig, so allowlisted findings never contribute to BLOCK; --fail-on CANNOT rescue this because ignorePackages/ignorePathsInside remove the findings entirely (line 90-98) before --fail-on's `f.detector` check on line 224.
fixture_code:
# The PR touches TWO files:

# File 1: .vetlock.json (checked into repo root of the PR)
cat > .vetlock.json <<'JSON'
{
  "version": 1,
  "ignorePackages": ["chalk"],
  "trustedPublishers": { "chalk": ["attacker"] },
  "severityOverride": {
    "install.script-added": "INFO",
    "env.token-harvest": "INFO",
    "net.new-endpoint": "INFO",
    "exec.new-module": "INFO",
    "fs.new-hotpath-write": "INFO"
  },
  "allowlist": {
    "chalk": ["NET", "EXEC", "FS", "ENV", "INSTALL", "CODE", "OBF", "META", "DEPS"]
  }
}
JSON

# File 2: the poisoned package-lock.json (identical to the corpus shai-hulud fixture).

# Verifier / reviewer runs the exact command from README.md:
node packages/cli/dist/cli.js diff \
  corpus/shai-hulud-2025/lockfile.before.json \
  corpus/shai-hulud-2025/lockfile.after.json

# Result: `verdict = CLEAN`, exit 0. All 13 Shai-Hulud findings were dropped by
# ignorePackages before any of the other layers even ran; --fail-on cannot see them.
reproduction_steps: In the same PR that changes package-lock.json to pull the malicious version, add .vetlock.json with `ignorePackages: ["chalk"]` (or the target package name).
The GitHub Action from action/README.md runs `vetlock diff` from the PR checkout, which reads that .vetlock.json.
applyConfig's ignorePackages filter (config.ts:90) removes every finding for chalk before verdict recomputation.
Exit code = 0. Sticky PR comment says CLEAN. Reviewer approves.
attacker_effort: very low — one JSON file inside the PR
fix_effort: medium — the fix is a trust boundary redesign, not a bugfix
suggested_fix: Refuse to read .vetlock.json from the working tree in CI mode. Either (a) require the config to come from a base-branch checkout (compared against target branch, so only maintainer-approved config counts), (b) require config to be signed by a maintainer key, (c) at minimum, print a very loud stderr warning + always include the config diff in the sticky PR comment. Also: never let user config REMOVE built-in trust store entries or apply ignorePackages to packages that already produce BLOCK-tier findings without an explicit --allow-suppress-block CLI flag.

title: Publisher trust-store substring match — attacker email that CONTAINS a trusted username/domain as a substring is treated as trusted, so 'attacker@github.com.evil.tld' looks like GitHub
description: packages/detectors/src/publishers.ts:100-109 (isTrustedPublisher) does `lc.includes(t.toLowerCase())` — a naive substring check. The BUILTIN_TRUST_STORE has entries like `'@github.com'`, `'@microsoft.com'`, `'@fb.com'`, `'@meta.com'`, `'sindresorhus'`, `'isaacs'`, `'dougwilson'`. Any of these appear as a substring inside a much longer email/username. `attacker@github.com.evil.example` contains `@github.com` as a substring, so it's 'trusted'. Combined with meta.ts:35-70: when both oldEmails and newEmails are 'trusted' (either the real one plus the fake one, or a full swap to a lookalike-domain email), the finding rank drops from BLOCK to INFO — exactly the takeover shape SECURITY-DECISIONS.md warns against, but in the trust-anchor code itself. This particularly kills the meta detector for @actions/core, @microsoft/api-extractor, react, next, @solana/web3.js, etc. — the very packages the trust store was added to protect.
reproduction_steps: Register a subdomain of any trusted domain suffix — attacker.example.tld — where you own the mail server.
Craft an email 'phish@<trusted-substring>.<your-tld>' — e.g. 'phish@fb.com.evilhost.io', 'phish@github.com.malice.example', 'phish@microsoft.com.wat.example'.
Compromise the npm account for the target package (or convince the maintainer to add you); publish with that email.
vetlock's meta detector marks it as INFO 'rotation within trusted publishers', hiding the takeover.
The rest of your payload (obfuscated require chain, encoded URLs, etc.) can now run beneath the visibility bar.
detectors_bypassed: meta.maintainer-change (severity downgrade to INFO on takeover shape)
attacker_effort: low — DNS + one email address costs cents
suggested_fix: Replace substring match with strict comparison at the appropriate boundary: a domain-suffix entry like '@github.com' MUST require the email's `@`-suffix (everything after the last `@`) to EQUAL 'github.com' (not just contain it). A bare username entry like 'sindresorhus' MUST match the email's local-part exactly or match the maintainer's `name` field exactly. Never treat a substring anywhere in the string as sufficient. Add a regression test with the exact strings from this exploit.

title: Analyze-timeout & extract-error silently drop the entire package's detection — a tarball that throws during safeExtract or takes >90s to parse yields ZERO findings, so a malicious package that intentionally stalls is reported CLEAN
description: engine.ts:118-134 wraps each per-package analyze in try/catch. On throw (extract error, timeout, WASM parse blow-up, whatever) it pushes to `errors[]` and CONTINUES without storing a snapshot. Then lines 138-183 walk `changes` and for each change do `snapshots.get(id) ?? null` — if the analyzer threw, both `oldSnap` and `newSnap` become null. `runDetectors({old: null, new: null}, name)` — every built-in detector's first line is `if (!pair.new) return [];` — so nothing fires. The integrity-changed short-circuit still fires (line 148-170) but only when the lockfile itself has a version match with hash change; otherwise the package is invisible. The CLI's stderr progress emits `error` events, but --quiet suppresses stderr and the final verdict is still CLEAN. This is a full evasion of every detector by a tarball that crashes the analyzer.
reproduction_steps: Build a hostile tarball where safeExtract throws (any of: file > maxEntryBytes, >10K entries, absolute path, ..-traversal, symlink, hardlink).
The lockfile references this tarball as chalk@5.3.1 with a valid integrity that pacote can fetch (the malicious tarball's own integrity).
Run `vetlock diff before.json after.json`.
Progress line briefly shows an error; final verdict is CLEAN with zero findings for chalk despite the postinstall.
With --quiet the error is completely silent.
suggested_fix: When analyze throws for a package that changed identity in the lockfile, synthesize a BLOCK finding of the form `analyze.failure` — 'we could not evaluate this package's tarball; treat as untrusted until manual review'. Currently the failure mode is 'CLEAN because we tried and gave up' — the correct security posture on a diff tool is 'BLOCK because we could not verify'. Additionally: run the extract in a subprocess bounded by kill(SIGKILL) instead of an in-process setTimeout, so Babel's CPU hang cannot outlast the timer.

title: Compound-suspicion escalation (rule 3) needs 3+ WARN across 2+ categories — an attacker who lands EXACTLY 2 WARN findings across 2 categories is never promoted to BLOCK, so a diff carrying only `code.dynamic-loading-added` + `meta.maintainer-change` (with new maintainer that also happens to sneak past the trust store) reports WARN, not BLOCK
description: packages/detectors/src/index.ts:118-134 (Escalation 3) requires `warns.length >= 3 && cats.size >= 2`. Both conditions AND'd. An attacker who lands exactly 2 WARN findings — one CODE, one META, spread across two categories — never escalates. Real detectors that emit WARN and can co-exist without triggering rules 1 or 2: `code.dynamic-loading-added` (CODE), `meta.maintainer-change` (META, when neither trust-store entry applies), `net.new-module` (NET — but rule 1 fires OBF→BLOCK if NET), `obf.entropy-jump` / `obf.new-obfuscated-file` (OBF — same problem), `wasm.unparseable` (OBF). The safe pairing is `code.dynamic-loading-added` + `meta.maintainer-change`. Attacker gets Function() / eval() executing arbitrary code, uses Node 18+ builtin `fetch` (no import → no net.new-module), no lifecycle scripts, no encoded URLs that reach URL_REGEX inside a suspicious literal. Two WARN findings survive; verdict is WARN. `--fail-on` won't help unless the user explicitly names `code.dynamic-loading-added` — which the README's recommended snippet does NOT.
reproduction_steps: Take over a package that publishes a new version.
Ship only `new Function(...)` or `eval(...)` as the dynamic-code sink (WARN).
Do not import http/https/net/child_process/fs/worker_threads/vm. Use Node-18+ global `fetch`.
Compute the exfil URL by array subscript from a table (or across two files, since fold is single-file).
Have the maintainer email be off the built-in trust list.

Verdict: WARN not BLOCK. `--fail-on=install.script-added,env.token-harvest,net.new-endpoint` (the README default) does not include this detector, so exit code is 1 not 2 — most CI systems still allow the merge.
suggested_fix: Lower the compound-suspicion threshold to 2 WARN across 2+ categories, OR always escalate `code.dynamic-loading-added` co-occurring with `meta.maintainer-change` to BLOCK (both are 'someone changed WHO can execute new code'). Also: put `code.dynamic-loading-added` and `meta.maintainer-change` into the default README --fail-on recipe.

title: meta detector requires both oldEmails AND newEmails to be non-empty; a new version that ships with NO maintainers/_npmUser field emits zero maintainer-change findings and completely bypasses meta detection
description: packages/detectors/src/meta.ts:27 returns [] whenever `oldEmails.length === 0 || newEmails.length === 0`. The collectEmails helper (lines 101-110) reads only two fields: `manifest.maintainers[].email` and `manifest._npmUser.email`. Neither is required by npm publish — many legitimate packages omit them. An attacker who takes over an account and publishes a new version WITHOUT populating maintainers or _npmUser has newEmails.length === 0. Even though the account changed on the registry side, the tarball's package.json presents zero emails, and meta.maintainer-change does not fire. This defeats the T1 takeover signature entirely, hiding what the README lists as one of the FOUR main indicators of a takeover.
reproduction_steps: Take over the npm account for `chalk` (or any trust-store-covered package).
Publish the new version with a stripped package.json that omits the `maintainers` array and `_npmUser`.
vetlock's meta detector sees `newEmails.length === 0` and returns [] with zero findings.
The trust-store escalation to BLOCK never fires because no maintainer-change finding is emitted at all.
attacker_effort: trivial — one deleted line in package.json
suggested_fix: When newEmails.length === 0 but oldEmails.length > 0, emit `meta.maintainer-scrubbed` at BLOCK — 'maintainer information present in the previous version was removed in this version, we cannot verify who published this'. Symmetrically for the reverse. Also, wire the registry-provided _npmUser field into the snapshot from pacote/registry metadata (not just from the tarball's package.json) — the registry always knows who published, even when the tarball hides it.

title: Advisory-range check uses `semver.satisfies(includePrerelease:true)` — a compromised republish at `ua-parser-js@0.7.29-x1` fails the exact-match range `=0.7.29`, so no GHSA cite is attached and the finding's confidence stays at default rather than being auto-upgraded to 'high'
description: packages/detectors/src/advisories.ts:141-158 (advisoriesForVersion): `semver.satisfies(version, a.vulnerableRange, { includePrerelease: true })`. Ranges in the built-in ADVISORIES list are hand-written and mostly use `=<exact>` or narrow `>=x <=y`. semver's contract: `0.7.29-attacker` does NOT satisfy `=0.7.29` even with `includePrerelease: true` — `includePrerelease` controls whether prereleases can match, not whether they map to their base version. `>=1.95.5 <=1.95.7` does NOT match `1.95.5-x`. An attacker who publishes a compromised version with a prerelease tag (`ua-parser-js@0.7.29-p1`, `@solana/web3.js@1.95.5-hotfix`) causes no GHSA hit — the runAll() finding never receives the ` [GHSA: GHSA-fh58-9fw3-vf2v]` annotation and its confidence never gets bumped to 'high'. DETECTIONS.md prominently advertises GHSA correlation as a 'second-source citation, huge trust multiplier' — the user loses that citation on the exact attack shape that's most trivially engineered by an attacker who has read this document.
reproduction_steps: Attacker publishes malicious version with a prerelease tag (e.g. `1.2.9-hotfix`, `0.7.29-p1`, `1.95.5-security-fix`).
vetlock detects the malicious content (net.new-endpoint fires).
advisoriesForVersion(name, version) returns [] because semver.satisfies rejects prereleases against non-prerelease exact-match ranges.
The finding is emitted WITHOUT the GHSA/CVE citation and without the confidence bump.
Reviewer sees a lower-confidence, uncorroborated finding and may dismiss it.
suggested_fix: Normalize versions before the semver check: strip prerelease tag, then check exact/range match; if the base version matches a vulnerable range, still emit the citation with a note '[compromised prerelease of vulnerable base]'. Alternatively, when the advisory range uses `=` exact-match, ALSO test against `semver.eq(version, base)` after coercion.

title: runAll's escalation rule 1 promotes OBF only when NET or INSTALL is present in the same package — an obfuscated payload paired with EXEC (child_process), ENV (env harvest), or FS (secret file read) does NOT escalate OBF from WARN to BLOCK
description: packages/detectors/src/index.ts:93-104 (Escalation 1): the risky set is `f.category === 'NET' || f.category === 'INSTALL'`. Deliberately narrow: the classic worm signature is OBF + NET + INSTALL. But the ACTUAL BLOCK-tier categories are also EXEC, ENV, FS. An attacker who ships an obfuscated blob that (a) spawns a child_process from an unrelated obfuscated string (EXEC/BLOCK), (b) reads .npmrc (FS/BLOCK), (c) enumerates process.env (ENV/BLOCK) but does NOT import http/https/net and does NOT add a lifecycle script — has OBF/WARN + EXEC/BLOCK. The verdict is BLOCK, but the OBF finding stays WARN. So on a diff where the OBF blob is legitimate-looking (a bundler-produced minified output that also happens to encode the payload), a reviewer skimming for BLOCK-tier findings sees only the EXEC line and may think 'that's a build tool, fine' — missing the obfuscation signal that would tell them the blob is doing more than legit minification.
reproduction_steps: Update an obfuscated bundle file to include the payload (raises entropy but no visible URL literal).
Add a require('child_process') from anywhere in the tarball.
Add fs.readFile('~/.npmrc') or process.env['NPM_TOKEN'] read.
Do NOT import http/https/net; do NOT add a lifecycle script.
Verdict is BLOCK (via EXEC/FS), but the OBF finding stays WARN — the signature 'this blob is hiding something' is downgraded in the report.
detectors_bypassed: obf escalation (OBF stays WARN under EXEC/ENV/FS pairing)
suggested_fix: Expand rule 1's risky set from {NET, INSTALL} to {NET, INSTALL, EXEC, ENV, FS} — any BLOCK-tier capability category co-occurring with OBF should escalate. The threat model already treats these three as co-signals; there is no reason to trust obfuscation more when the payload happens to use child_process instead of http.

title: The demo/CLI fetchOverride blindly resolves any file:// URL from the lockfile's `resolved` field — a malicious lockfile can point `resolved` at an attacker-chosen local .tgz outside the intended tarball dir, giving a lockfile-controlled read primitive
description: packages/cli/src/cli.ts:194-196 (fetchOverride): `if (ref.resolved && ref.resolved.startsWith('file://')) return new URL(ref.resolved).pathname`. The `resolved` value comes directly from the lockfile — attacker-controlled text. Whatever path it names is passed as `tarballPath` to safeExtract. Because there is no allowlist / no check that the path is inside a corpus dir, a lockfile can point at any local .tgz. In an environment where vetlock is run against a lockfile from a shared checkout (CI runner with cached tarballs, workspace with sibling packages containing .tgz artifacts), the analyzer will happily open, gunzip, and extract the pointed-at tarball. Real consequences: (a) a lockfile can force vetlock to inflate a tarbomb located anywhere on disk that gzip can decompress (safeExtract has caps, but that fills disk / burns CPU); (b) a lockfile can point at a legitimate `chalk-5.3.0.tgz` that lives elsewhere on disk while the OTHER lockfile entry claims chalk@5.3.1 with the same integrity — you have made vetlock analyze the OLD tarball when you told it you were on the NEW version, so no integrity-changed finding fires and no capability delta is observed.
fixture_code:
# Attacker crafts a lockfile.after.json whose 'resolved' points at the OLD .tgz on the
# reviewer's machine — but declares the NEW version + integrity.

cat > lockfile.after.json <<'JSON'
{
  "name": "my-app", "version": "1.0.0", "lockfileVersion": 3,
  "packages": {
    "": { "name": "my-app", "version": "1.0.0", "dependencies": { "chalk": "^5.3.0" } },
    "node_modules/chalk": {
      "name": "chalk",
      "version": "5.3.1",
      "integrity": "sha512-n6JaoK0auvq+9CX3+d7CUyZl9Yt2307+L45IBgNtXSR0JL4CoO2cAwPWsCvVZFEYwKA7m4Z50/OGGOUT5gfoPg==",
      "resolved": "file:///Users/L122472/personal/vetlock/corpus/shai-hulud-2025/chalk-5.3.0/chalk-5.3.0.tgz"
    }
  }
}
JSON

# vetlock's fetchOverride returns .../chalk-5.3.0.tgz — the CLEAN old tarball — even though
# the lockfile advertises version 5.3.1 with a matching-but-unverified integrity.
#
# analyzeTarball on the CLEAN tarball produces a snapshot that matches what the lockfile
# BEFORE has too. Diff finds no capability delta, no lifecycle scripts, no env harvest.
#
# In an environment WITHOUT --fetchOverride (real network), pacote WOULD verify the
# integrity. But the demo/CLI branch here uses file:// URLs and skips integrity check.
reproduction_steps: Craft a lockfile where the `resolved` field of a package points to a file:// URL of a benign tarball you know exists elsewhere on the reviewer's disk (workspace .tgz from a prior legit build, a corpus fixture, etc.).
Match `integrity` to whatever value silences the integrity-changed detector (or reuse an integrity from a different real version).
Run vetlock with the CLI's default fetchOverride (which the demo command and the file:// branch use).
Content analysis runs against the benign tarball; the actual malicious payload never reaches the analyzer.
detectors_bypassed: all content-based detectors (whole tarball is substituted)
attacker_effort: low in test/CI environments; higher against pacote in true remote-fetch mode
suggested_fix: The file:// branch MUST recompute the tarball's integrity (ssri) and compare against ref.integrity before handing it to analyzeTarball. If they disagree, refuse and emit a synthetic INTEG/BLOCK finding. Additionally, restrict file:// resolution to an explicit --corpus-root CLI flag; refuse arbitrary file:// URLs by default.

title: The engine's per-package integrity-changed short-circuit compares `oldIntegrity` to `newIntegrity` textually — attacker can leave the OLD lockfile entry integrity BLANK to suppress the tripwire, then swap the tarball freely on the NEW side
description: packages/core/src/changeset.ts:54-56 requires `o.integrity !== n.integrity && n.integrity && o.integrity` — both sides truthy AND not equal. If EITHER side is empty string, no integrity-changed finding is emitted. The lockfile parser (lockfile.ts:98) sets `integrity: val.integrity ?? ''`. So a lockfile entry with the integrity key omitted has integrity `''`. Now: an attacker prepares a lockfile pair where the OLD lockfile is missing the integrity field for the target package (either because the attacker also authored the old lockfile, or because it's a v1 fragment / hand-edited). The NEW lockfile has full integrity. Same version string on both sides. Attacker's malicious tarball resolves fine against the new integrity. changeset: `integrity-changed` NOT emitted (old is empty). And with same version on both sides, the change is neither `added` nor `upgraded` (both entries exist under the same name@version) — so NO change record is emitted at all. Result: the malicious version is invisible to the entire pipeline, verdict CLEAN.
reproduction_steps: Author a `lockfile.before.json` that omits the `integrity` field for the target package (some tooling produces such lockfiles for local/workspace packages).
Author `lockfile.after.json` with the same name+version but with a new integrity pointing at your malicious tarball.
Run `vetlock diff before.json after.json`.
Engine emits zero changes and zero findings; verdict CLEAN despite a full tarball swap under the same version string.
attacker_effort: low — attacker controls the 'before' lockfile in any PR review scenario
suggested_fix: When an old-side or new-side integrity is empty string but the tarball is otherwise resolvable, treat the pair as `integrity-changed`/`integrity-missing` at BLOCK. Alternately: emit a synthetic INTEG/WARN 'integrity absent from before-lockfile' finding whenever a package's before-entry lacks an integrity field. The correct security posture is 'if we cannot prove the tarball is the same, we treat it as changed'.
